papers.title: High velocity outflows from young star-forming galaxies in the UKIDSS Ultra-Deep Survey
papers.author_names: E. J. Bradshaw O. Almaini W. G. Hartley K. T. Smith C. J. Conselice J. S. Dunlop C. Simpson R. W. Chuter M. Cirasuolo S. Foucaud R. J. McLure A. Mortlock H. Pearce

abstract.text: We investigate galactic-scale outflows in the redshift range 0.71 < z < 1.63, using 413 K-band selected galaxies observed in the spectroscopic follow-up of the UKIDSS Ultra-Deep Survey (UDSz). The galaxies have an average stellar mass of ~10^9.5 solar masses and span a wide range in rest-frame colours, representing typical star-forming galaxies at this epoch. We stack the spectra by various galaxy properties, including stellar mass, [OII] equivalent width, star-formation rate, specific star-formation rate and rest-frame spectral indices. We find that outflows are present in virtually all spectral stacks, with velocities ranging from 100-1000 km s^-1, indicating that large-scale outflowing winds are a common property at these redshifts. The highest velocity outflows (>500 km s^-1) are found in galaxies with the highest stellar masses and the youngest stellar populations. Our findings suggest that high velocity galactic outflows are mostly driven by star-forming processes rather than AGN, with implied mass outflow rates comparable to the rates of star formation. Such behaviour is consistent with models required to reproduce the high-redshift mass-metallicity relation.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: high-velocity or extreme-velocity stars

brief.tldr: [observational astronomy research paper] The study identifies high-velocity galactic outflows (100–1000 km s⁻¹) in 413 K-band selected star-forming galaxies at 0.71 ≤ z ≤ 1.63, with the most extreme outflows (>500 km s⁻¹) occurring in high-mass, young-star-population galaxies. Outflows are predominantly driven by star formation (not AGN), with mass outflow rates comparable to star formation rates, supporting models that link galactic winds to galaxy quenching and the formation of the red sequence at high redshift.

source_fields.title: High velocity outflows from young star-forming galaxies in the UKIDSS Ultra-Deep Survey

source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Discussion: The study finds that star-forming galaxies at high redshift exhibit mass outflow rates of ~45 $M_{\\odot}$ yr$^{-1}$, comparable to their star formation rates, with outflow velocities up to 1000 km s$^{-1}$ driven by starbursts rather than AGN activity, indicating that outflows play a key role in gas processing and galaxy evolution. Conclusions: This study finds that strong galactic outflows are ubiquitous in star-forming galaxies at $0.71<z<1.63$, driven by stellar feedback rather than AGN activity, with outflow velocities up to $\\sim1000$ km s$^{-1}$ and mass outflow rates comparable to star formation rates, supporting models of high-redshift galaxy evolution. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel method to detect and quantify high-velocity galactic outflows (exceeding 1000 km s⁻¹) in starburst and AGN-driven galaxies using UV absorption lines, addressing the unresolved problem of how galactic winds drive galaxy quenching and shape the red sequence in the early Universe. Appendix A Fors2: This section describes the methodology for analyzing FORS2 Mgii absorption profiles, including a two-component fit that reveals outflows at approximately 320 km s⁻¹, and presents stacked analyses of galaxies grouped by physical properties to investigate variations in outflow characteristics. Sample Selection: This section describes the selection and characteristics of galaxies in the Ultra Deep Survey (UDS), including deep photometric and spectroscopic data from multiple instruments, used to study galaxy outflows and their velocity structures. Creating Composite Spectra: This section describes the creation and validation of a high signal-to-noise composite spectrum from 341 VIMOS spectra, using the Mgii doublet as a tracer of galactic outflows, with careful calibration checks and stacking methods ensuring reliability. Outflows in Galaxies Stacked by Property: This section analyzes the relationship between Mgii outflows and galaxy properties, finding no correlation between [Oii] equivalent width (a star formation indicator) and the equivalent width of Mgii outflows, despite observing higher outflow velocities in galaxies with strong star formation.

abstract: The highest velocity outflows (>500 km s^-1) are found in galaxies with the highest stellar masses and the youngest stellar populations.
Our findings suggest that high velocity galactic outflows are mostly driven by star-forming processes rather than AGN, with implied mass outflow rates comparable to the rates of star formation.
Such behaviour is consistent with models required to reproduce the high-redshift mass-metallicity relation.
title: High velocity outflows from young star-forming galaxies in the UKIDSS Ultra-Deep Survey

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper studies galactic-scale outflows from galaxies, not individual stellar objects. Focus is on galaxy properties, outflow velocities, and star formation feedback mechanisms.
